# Signal-to-Value Pipeline

Most manufactured traction follows a recurring chain.

| Stage | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Proxy chosen | Choose a signal outsiders trust | views, reviews, stars, upvotes, installs, ARR |
| 2. Signal engineered | Create, amplify, distort, or launder the signal | clippers, fake reviews, engagement pods, bots, metric inflation |
| 3. Origin disguised | Make the signal look independent or organic | UGC camouflage, hidden sponsorship, anonymous accounts |
| 4. System triggered | Platform/audience/investor reacts to the signal | recommendations, rankings, press, funding conversations |
| 5. Value extracted | Convert the apparent traction into money or status | sales, royalties, installs, fundraising, brand deals |
| 6. Signal laundering | Later real attention makes the original distortion harder to identify | a manufactured trend becomes a real trend |

## The laundering problem

Manufactured traction can become partially self-fulfilling. A fake trend can attract real viewers. A gamed launch rank can create real investor conversations. A fake review burst can lead to real sales. This does not erase the ethical issue; it makes it harder to prove after the fact.

## Research implication

Researchers should examine the origin, timing, and coordination of early signals, not only final metrics.
